narratives.executive_summary: NGS ARR reached $8.78B as of 2026-07, up 57% year over year — of which $6.95B is organic and $1.83B is inorganic from the CyberArk and Chronosphere acquisitions. Net revenue retention of 120% and a Rule-of-40 score of 69 confirm growth and profitability are compounding together, with adjusted FCF margin at 37.5%. The business sits at 44% of the $20B FY2030 NGS ARR target with 2,400 platformized customers. On current driver-based trajectory the base case reaches $15.13B by FY2030 (76% of target); the bull case reaches $18.10B. Closing the remaining gap requires sustained above-trend execution: hitting $20B implies a ~23% CAGR, versus the base case's ~15% — the difference platformization, new-logo growth, and further M&A must bridge.
narratives.variance: In FY2026-Q4, NGS ARR of $8.78B came in 4.2% ahead of the board-approved plan ($8.43B), a $0.35B variance. Revenue was +4.2% vs plan and operating margin +40 bps. The ARR bridge shows expansion ($0.61B) as the largest positive motion, partly offset by churn ($-0.19B); net-new logos contributed $0.19B. The expansion-led bridge is the platformization flywheel working as designed — existing customers growing faster than new logos.
narratives.platformization: Platformized customers retain and expand materially better than single-product accounts — the data shows roughly 120% blended NRR, with platformized cohorts well above non-platformized. That gap is the entire economic case for spending on platformization incentives: each conversion buys a faster-compounding revenue stream. With 2,400 platformized customers today against a 4,000+ FY2030 goal, the incentive ROI model shows attractive NPV and short payback, and the tornado confirms value is overwhelmingly driven by the retention gap.
narratives.ma: Inorganic NGS ARR of $1.83B from CyberArk and Chronosphere is now 21% of total NGS ARR. Separating it out, organic growth remains the core engine while M&A adds identity and observability adjacencies. The accretion/dilution model illustrates the classic corp-dev tension: a high-growth tuck-in can be near-term EPS-dilutive yet strongly NPV-positive — justifiable on strategic and DCF grounds even when it pressures next year's EPS.
narratives.threat: External threat signals — CVE volume, disclosed breaches, and an AI-threat index — do improve the near-term demand forecast. In a like-for-like 1-step ablation, adding lagged threat features changed forecast WAPE by 17% and the augmented model beat the seasonal-naive baseline. The lead is ~2 months, so the signal helps the next quarter's demand read but cannot inform the multi-year $20B trajectory — an honest, bounded result.
